era_context: The mid-12th century CE saw the Crusader States in the Levant under sustained pressure from Ayyubid expansion under Saladin's rise to power. In Europe, the High Middle Ages were marked by feudal consolidation, the growth of cathedral schools and early universities, and intensifying conflicts between secular and ecclesiastical authority. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controlled northern China while the Southern Song Dynasty ruled the south; in Japan, the Heian period was transitioning toward the Kamakura shogunate. The Islamic world experienced significant cultural and scientific flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for most regions)
Northern Europe beyond Scandinavia and the Baltic (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (reliant on fragmentary sources and later compilations)

events.title: Battle of the Horns of Hattin — Saladin defeats Crusader forces
events.description: Saladin (Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub) defeated a major Crusader army led by Guy of Lusignan and Reynald of Châtillon near the Horns of Hattin. This decisive victory broke Crusader military dominance in the Levant and led directly to the fall of Jerusalem in 1187. The battle is considered a turning point in the Crusades, demonstrating the effectiveness of Ayyubid military organization and Saladin's strategic acumen. The Crusaders' defeat was partly due to poor logistics, internal division, and Saladin's superior cavalry tactics.

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of the Levant (Fath al-Quds)
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin (al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya)

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary Islamic and Crusader chronicles. Dating to July 1187 is secure, though some sources place it in 1186 or 1188 depending on calendar conversion.

events.title: Death of Emperor Henry II of England
events.description: Henry II, King of England and Duke of Normandy and Anjou, died at Chinon Castle on 6 July 1189 (not 1161; this entry is corrected below). Henry II was one of the most powerful rulers of 12th-century Europe, controlling vast territories in France and England. His reign saw the development of common law, the expansion of royal justice, and conflicts with the Church (notably the murder of Thomas Becket in 1170). His death marked the end of an era of strong Angevin rule and led to succession disputes among his sons.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, Duke of Normandy and Anjou)
events.certainty_note: Henry II died in 1189, not 1161. This entry is included to note a significant error in the research prompt or source data. No major event of comparable significance is reliably dated to 1161 in England.

events.title: Jin Dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Jin Dynasty, established by the Jurchen people in 1115, continued to consolidate its control over northern China during the 1160s. The Jin maintained a dual administrative system incorporating both Jurchen and Han Chinese bureaucratic traditions. This period saw relative stability in the north, though the Jin faced ongoing pressure from the Southern Song Dynasty to the south and from Mongol tribes to the north. The Jin's military strength derived from its cavalry forces and the integration of conquered Han Chinese territories.

sources.name: Jurchen Jin Dynasty records (Jurchenic and Chinese sources)

events.certainty_note: The Jin Dynasty's political consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records. 1161 marks the accession of Emperor Shizong, a significant administrative transition, though no single dramatic event defines the year.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Hattin is traditionally dated to 1187, not 1161. This is a critical error in the research parameters. No major historical event of comparable significance is reliably documented for 1161 CE. The year 1161 itself is relatively underdocumented in European sources, with most major events of the period occurring in adjacent years (1160, 1162, 1187).
historiographic_note: The year 1161 CE falls in a period of moderate documentation for Europe and the Islamic world, but relatively sparse for most other regions. European sources are dominated by monastic chronicles and royal records, creating a Western bias. Islamic sources are richer for the Levant and Egypt but thinner for Central Asia and the Steppe. Chinese sources are abundant but often focus on court politics rather than broader social change. The lack of a clearly significant event for 1161 suggests either that major events occurred in adjacent years or that the historical record for this particular year is genuinely sparse.

graph_edges.note: Saladin's victory at Hattin (if dated to 1187) directly enabled the conquest of Jerusalem
Jin Dynasty consolidation was part of a longer trajectory ending in Mongol conquest
